title: Algorithmic Stochastic Localization for the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick Model
speaker: Mark Sellke
abstract: Sampling from high-dimensional, multimodal distributions is a computationally challenging and fundamental task.  This talk will focus on a family of random instances of such problems described by random quadratic potentials on the hypercube known as the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick model.  I will describe an approximate sampling algorithm for the high temperature regime as well as matching low-temperature hardness results stemming from disorder chaos.  Our algorithm uses stochastic localization, which progressively tilts the desired measure towards a single configuration, together

proof_stages.label: SK Gibbs sampling target
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the algorithmic problem of sampling a high-dimensional Gibbs measure, recalls Ising/Glauber sampling, and specializes to the GOE Sherrington-Kirkpatrick model with constant inverse temperature.

proof_stages.label: Stochastic-localization reduction to posterior means
proof_stages.summary: Sampling from a target measure is reformulated as simulating the averaged diffusion Y_t=B_t+tX_* whose drift is the posterior mean of X_* given the current endpoint, so a discretized sampler needs repeated conditional-mean estimates.

proof_stages.label: High-temperature Wasserstein sampling theorem
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing the expected beta=1 threshold and prior total-variation mixing results for smaller beta, the speaker states the main result: for every beta<1, an efficient algorithm samples the SK Gibbs measure up to arbitrarily small normalized Wasserstein error with high probability over the GOE matrix.

proof_stages.label: Stability barrier beyond beta equals one
proof_stages.summary: The algorithm's stability under small perturbations of the coupling matrix implies stability of the true Gibbs measures, while disorder chaos plus replica-symmetry breaking show that no similarly stable algorithm can sample past beta>1.

proof_stages.label: TAP/AMP construction of the denoiser
proof_stages.summary: To compute the posterior mean needed by localization, the speaker derives a TAP-corrected mean-field fixed point with the localization observation as an external field and turns it into a Bolthausen-style approximate-message-passing recursion.

proof_stages.label: State evolution and MMSE certification
proof_stages.summary: The AMP recursion is analyzed by a contiguity change to a spiked Gaussian matrix model and by state evolution, giving an explicit mean-squared-error curve that is certified optimal via an MMSE area law.

proof_stages.label: Refinement, assembly, and scope of the sampler
proof_stages.summary: A local strong-convexity refinement upgrades the rough AMP estimate to the accuracy needed for the discretized localization sampler, after which the speaker compares the denoising structure with diffusion models and summarizes the beta<1 result and its limits in related models.
